summary: In this interview, scholar M M McCabe discusses the philosophical legacy of Socrates through five recommended books. The text explores Socrates' method of rigorous questioning, his insistence on recognizing one's own ignorance, and his ultimate focus on the question of 'How best to live?', while also examining how his ideas and persona were represented by Plato, Aristophanes, and modern scholars.

principles.statement: True wisdom is knowing that you are not wise.
principles.rationale: Many people pretend to have knowledge because they know a specific craft or have been inspired, but they fail the true conditions of knowledge. Wisdom begins with recognizing the limits of your own understanding.
principles.source_quote: he concedes only that he has the wisdom of knowing that he’s not wise.

principles.statement: Death is preferable to giving up philosophy.
principles.rationale: A life does not derive its value simply from lasting a long time, but from the things within it that make it valuable. Giving up the pursuit of wisdom to merely survive renders life empty.
principles.source_quote: death is preferable to giving up philosophy.

principles.statement: I'd rather die than do injustice.
principles.rationale: Maintaining moral integrity and adhering to just principles is more important than preserving one's own life.
principles.source_quote: I’d rather die than do injustice

frameworks.name: Socratic Midwifery
frameworks.when_to_apply: When examining the views, assertions, and principles of others to determine if their ideas are sound.
steps: Claim ignorance on the subject at hand.
Ask the interlocutor to teach you, positioning them as the expert.
Check and recheck the basis for their argument, assertion, and principle.
Follow the argument where it leads to determine if the idea is real or empty.
frameworks.source_quote: he deals with people who are ‘pregnant’ in mind, and figures out whether they have real ideas or ‘wind eggs’

mental_models.name: The Midwife of Ideas
mental_models.description: Socrates views himself not as a creator of theories, but as a midwife who helps others give birth to their thoughts, testing them to see if they are viable ('real ideas') or empty ('wind eggs').
mental_models.example: Engaging with interlocutors to draw out their definitions of knowledge, rather than proposing his own theories.

mental_models.name: Socratic Irony
mental_models.description: Feigning ignorance to force argumentative clarity from an interlocutor. It involves saying what one means and doesn't mean simultaneously, creating a gap that requires philosophical negotiation to uncover the truth.
mental_models.example: Saying to a pompous expert like Euthyphro, 'teach me, because I don't know anything about this subject in hand, but I am sure you know, so you can put me right...'

heuristics: Follow the argument where it leads.
Ask not 'What should I do now?', but 'How best to live?'

signature_quotes.text: I’d rather die than do injustice
signature_quotes.context: Describing Socrates' few determinate views and general principles.
The deeper, central question of Socratic philosophy, shifting the scope from immediate actions to whole lives.

signature_quotes.text: teach me, because I don't know anything about this subject in hand, but I am sure you know, so you can put me right...
signature_quotes.context: An example of Socratic irony used when addressing an interlocutor who claims to be an expert.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming that expertise in a specific craft translates to general wisdom about everything else.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It leads to a false pretense of knowledge, causing people to fail the true conditions of wisdom, which require recognizing one's own ignorance.

anti_patterns.description: Valuing a long, dull life over a life filled with the things that make it valuable.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Ethical theories and lives are empty and void unless you can explain what it is for a life to actually have value; mere duration is insufficient.
